attributes.stem: An 80-year-old man with Parkinson disease is reviewed in his general practice. He was diagnosed six years ago on the basis of the clinical findings of a classical resting tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia. He has been taking a levodopa/carbidopa combination twice a day. Over the last two months, he has noticed that his tremor has worsened and only improves once he takes his next dose of the medication. At the same time, he has noticed that for about 30 minutes after taking this dose, he has increased and uncontrolled movements of his arms.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate approach to management?
options: Smaller and more frequent dosing of levodopa/carbidopa
attributes.explanation:
This patient is experiencing two symptoms: wearing-off effect and post-dose dyskinesia. The wearing-off effect occurs when the medication's effect diminishes before the next dose, leading to a return of parkinsonian symptoms. Dyskinesia refers to involuntary, erratic, writhing movements of the face, arms, legs, or trunk, often occurring when dopamine levels are high. The most effective way to manage these symptoms is to provide smaller and more frequent dosing of levodopa/carbidopa, which helps maintain a more consistent dopamine level in the brain, reducing both wearing-off and dyskinesia.

Increasing the dose of levodopa/carbidopa may reduce the wearing-off effect by providing more dopamine, but it is likely to exacerbate post-dose dyskinesia due to higher peak dopamine levels. Levodopa is converted to dopamine in the brain, improving motor symptoms in Parkinson disease, while carbidopa prevents peripheral conversion of levodopa, increasing its availability in the brain.

Rotigotine is a dopamine agonist that can be used as adjunctive therapy in Parkinson disease. It stimulates dopamine receptors directly, mimicking the action of dopamine in the brain. However, it may not effectively address the wearing-off effect or dyskinesia in this scenario, as it does not provide the same immediate increase in dopamine levels as levodopa.

Ceasing carbidopa would worsen the wearing-off effect and increase levodopa requirements, leading to more side effects. Carbidopa is essential as it inhibits the enzyme that converts levodopa to dopamine outside the brain, allowing more levodopa to reach the brain and reducing peripheral side effects.

Entacapone is a Catechol-O-methyltransferase (COMT) inhibitor that prevents the breakdown of levodopa in the periphery, prolonging its effect. While it may help improve the wearing-off effect by extending the duration of levodopa action, it could worsen post-dose dyskinesia by increasing peak dopamine levels.
learningPoints: Dyskinesia refers to involuntary, erratic, writhing movements of the face, arms, legs, or trunk.
Levodopa is converted to dopamine in the brain, improving motor symptoms in Parkinson disease and carbidopa prevents peripheral conversion of levodopa, increasing its availability in the brain.
references: Radhakrishnan DM, Goyal V. Parkinson's disease: A review. Neurol India. 2018;66(Supplement):S26–S35. doi:10.4103/0028-3886.226451
Beitz JM. Parkinson's disease: a review. Front Biosci (Schol Ed). 2014;6:65–74. Published 2014 Jan 1. doi:10.2741/s415

attributes.name: Junior
attributes.description: Suitable for a junior, pre-clinical medical student
